title: Creating an eBOM
description: Explore four methods for creating an eBOM in Windchill: Product Structure Browser, Auto Associate, Import from Spreadsheet, and Save As.

topics.title: eBOM Creation Methods Overview

content.text: Windchill provides four methods you can use individually or in combination to create an engineering bill of materials. The method you choose depends on the available data and your design approach.
Four Methods for Creating an eBOM

rows: Creating small to mid-size product structures manually
Linking CAD documents to Windchill parts automatically
Bulk loading large sets of parts and structures from external data
Creating product structures of similar type products by duplication

content.text: Case Study: ITC Auto Drill 720

The ITC drilling machine manufacturing company wants to automate their existing Drill 720 series product. With design and manufacturing facilities scattered across the globe, ITC has decided to follow a top-down design approach to launch the new version in a timely manner.

The case study follows five high-level process steps: Create the BOM, Create the Baseline, Edit the BOM, Manage the BOM, and Create the BOM Report. These steps will be covered across all modules in this course.

content.prompt: Match each eBOM creation method to its primary use case:

pairs.left: Manually add and remove parts in a structure
Automatically link CAD documents to Windchill parts
Bulk load large sets of parts from external data
Duplicate an existing product structure for similar products

keyTakeaways: Windchill provides four methods for creating an eBOM: PSB, Auto Associate, Import from Spreadsheet, and Save As.
The choice of method depends on available data, design approach, and product structure size.
Methods can be used individually or in combination to build a complete product structure.
The ITC case study follows a five-step process from BOM creation through reporting.

topics.title: PSB and Auto Associate

content.text: The Product Structure Browser (PSB) and Auto Associate are two primary methods for creating an eBOM. The PSB is used for direct manipulation of the part structure, while Auto Associate automates the linking of CAD documents to Windchill parts.
Using the PSB to Create an eBOM

The PSB can be used in both CAD-centric and part-centric environments and is typically used for creating small to mid-size product structures. With the PSB, you can add and remove Windchill parts and edit attribute values.

cards.back: Add a part that already exists in Windchill into the current product structure. Use the Editing group in the command ribbon or the right-click menu.
Create one or multiple new parts directly within the product structure. You can set the part type, end item status, number, and name.
Remove a part from the structure or edit its attributes, rename it, or move it to a different location in Windchill.

content.text: Using Auto Associate to Create an eBOM

Auto Associate automatically locates and associates an existing Windchill part to a CAD document, or creates a new part if no matching part exists. It is typically used in CAD-driven design workflows.

rows: Preferred method. Provides a better view of the association and greater control when creating structure for selective CAD documents.
Performed automatically or by selecting the Auto Associate option during check-in from the CAD tool. Creates product structure based on CAD structure.

content.text: Windchill uses the CAD document's base number as the default criteria to find a matching Windchill part. You can customize this behavior through the Auto Associate preferences in Windchill administration.

content.prompt: Arrange the steps for using Auto Associate from the workspace in order:
items: Check out the CAD assembly to the workspace
Select Edit > Auto Associate from the workspace action menu
Windchill locates matching parts or creates new ones
Review and confirm the associations between CAD documents and parts

keyTakeaways: The PSB is used for direct manipulation of product structures in both CAD-centric and part-centric environments.
PSB actions include inserting existing parts, creating new parts, removing parts, and editing attributes.
Auto Associate automatically links CAD documents to Windchill parts using the base number as matching criteria.
Auto Associate can be performed from the workspace action menu (preferred) or during check-in.

topics.title: Import from Spreadsheet and Save As

content.text: Import from Spreadsheet and Save As provide additional methods for creating product structures in Windchill. These methods are particularly useful for bulk loading data and duplicating existing structures.
Import from Spreadsheet

You can import part and product structure data from a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet into a library or product. This method is primarily used for bulk loading large sets of Windchill parts and product structures when definitions are maintained externally.
Out of the box, only a Windchill Product Manager has access to the Import from Spreadsheet action. Administrators may need to grant access to other roles.
Save As

The Save As method works best for creating product structures of similar type products. You can duplicate the complete structure or a smaller portion. If the associated CAD structure exists, you can gather it for duplication.

rows: Available to users with edit permissions
Does not import CAD documents directly
Can gather associated CAD structure for duplication

content.text: When using Save As, you can collect related parts, drawings, and CAD/Dynamic documents for the assembly. The new objects can be renamed during the Save As process.

content.prompt: Match each scenario to the best eBOM creation method:

pairs.left: Product definitions are maintained in an Excel file
A new product is very similar to an existing one
Building a small structure manually with specific parts
CAD models need to be automatically linked to parts

keyTakeaways: Import from Spreadsheet is used for bulk loading large sets of parts and structures from Excel files.
Only the Windchill Product Manager has default access to Import from Spreadsheet.
Save As duplicates existing product structures and optionally collects associated CAD documents.
Save As works best when creating new products that are similar to existing ones.

topics.title: Create an eBOM Using Save As

content.text: In this exercise, you use the Save As action to create a new eBOM for the Auto Drill 720 by duplicating an existing product structure. You will collect related parts and CAD documents, set the location, and verify the new product structure.
You should be signed in to Windchill as Mike Jones (mjones/ptc). Mike is the product manager for the ITC Auto Drill 720 team.

content.title: Create an eBOM Using the Save As Method
content.objective: Use Save As to duplicate the existing Drill 720 product structure into the Auto Drill 720 Series, including child parts and CAD documents.

tasks.title: Create an eBOM Using Save As

steps.action: Browse to the Auto Drill – 720 Series product folders.
steps.detail: Notice that it does not have an Engineering folder. You will copy contents from the existing Drill 720 Series.

steps.action: Go to the Drill – 720 Series product Engineering folder.
steps.detail: The remaining steps in this task will copy the contents of an existing assembly into the new Auto Drill – 720 Series.

steps.action: In the Search in table field, type Drill*asm.
steps.detail: This filters the folder contents to show only the CAD assembly documents.

steps.action: Click the View information icon for the DRILL_720.ASM CAD document.
steps.detail: This opens the Information page for the CAD assembly document.

steps.action: Click the Actions menu.
steps.detail: The Actions menu contains the Save As option for duplicating the assembly.

steps.action: Select the Save As option.
steps.detail: Save As allows you to duplicate the assembly and all its dependent objects into a new location.

steps.action: Click Dependents from the toolbar and select All.
steps.detail: This ensures all dependent objects in the assembly are included in the duplication.

steps.action: Select the Select all rows checkbox and click the Collect related parts icon.
steps.detail: This collects all the related Windchill parts for the assembly.

steps.action: Select the Select all rows checkbox and click the Collect related drawings icon.
steps.detail: This collects all related drawings for the assembly.

steps.action: Select the Select all rows checkbox, if not already selected, and click the Collect related CAD/Dynamic documents icon.
steps.detail: This collects all the CAD and Dynamic documents related to the assembly.

steps.action: Keep the default names for the new assembly.
steps.detail: Windchill will append '_New' to the names of all duplicated objects by default.
steps.hint: You could rename them during the Save As process if desired.

steps.action: Click the Set Location icon from the toolbar.
steps.detail: You need to specify where the new objects will be stored.

steps.action: In the Set Location dialog, click the Context menu and select the Auto Drill – 720 Series product option.
steps.detail: This sets the target product context for the new assembly.

steps.action: Click the Create New Folder icon to create a new folder for the assembly and its related parts.
steps.detail: The Auto Drill – 720 Series product needs an Engineering folder to store the new objects.

steps.action: In the New Folder dialog, type Engineering in the Name field.
steps.detail: This creates the Engineering folder in the Auto Drill – 720 Series product.

steps.action: Click OK to create the new folder.
steps.detail: The Engineering folder is now created in the Auto Drill – 720 Series product.

steps.action: Click OK to set the location.
steps.detail: The location is now set to the Engineering folder in the Auto Drill – 720 Series product.

steps.action: Click OK to create the new assembly structure in the Save As dialog.
steps.detail: Windchill creates copies of all selected objects with '_New' appended to their names.

steps.action: Go to the Auto Drill – 720 Series product Engineering folder.
steps.detail: All the parts have _New appended at the end of their names to distinguish them from the original Drill_720 parts.
steps.hint: You could have renamed them something else during the Save As process, if desired.

steps.action: Use the Search in table field to find the DRILL_720_NEW End Item part.
steps.detail: The search filters the folder contents to locate the new end item.

steps.action: Go to the DRILL_720_NEW End Item part Information page.
steps.detail: Click the part name or View information icon to open the Information page.

steps.action: Click the Structure tab and observe the product structure.
steps.detail: Verify that the complete product structure has been duplicated from the original Drill 720, including all child parts and CAD documents.

keyTakeaways: Save As duplicates an existing product structure including child parts, drawings, and CAD documents.
You must explicitly collect related parts, drawings, and CAD/Dynamic documents during Save As.
New objects created by Save As can be placed in a different product context and folder.
Always verify the new product structure after duplication to ensure completeness.
